companyCaseStudy.title: How Uber Scaled Service Discovery & MySQL Sharding with Ringpop + Schemaless — cutting tail latency and outages at hundreds of thousands of RPS
companyCaseStudy.summary: Uber needed a way to run thousands of microservices and millions of RPCs per second across many datacenters without brittle central coordination. They solved service discovery and hot-spotting by building Ringpop (a consistent-hash membership layer) and Schemaless (MySQL sharding + routing), driving big wins in failover time, throughput, and operational simplicity.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Uber's stack had a hard problem: thousands of services calling each other, at peaks of hundreds of thousands of RPCs/sec, across dozens of datacenters. Centralized service discovery and naive DB sharding were creating hotspots, long restart windows, and painful partial outages. Engineers needed something that scaled linearly and failed locally — not catastrophically.

## The Problem

- Scale: services issued hundreds of thousands of RPCs per second and the number of services and hosts was growing rapidly.
- Latency & tail risk: central lookups and long reconfiguration times produced high p99s and timeout storms during failures.
- Sharding pain: MySQL-based stores required manual shard routing; hot shards and cross-shard coordination caused operational complexity.

### Constraints & non-goals

- Wanted to avoid a heavyweight centralized coordinator (no single consul-like bottleneck).
- Wanted minimal changes to existing service code and MySQL usage.
- Needed something operationally debuggable by SREs and engineers.

## The Solution (High level)

Uber implemented two complementary systems:

1. Ringpop — a library (for multiple languages) that provides membership + consistent-hash routing in-process. By embedding the ring into each node, RPC clients can discover the owner of a key locally without a central lookup. It uses SWIM-style gossip for fast membership and consistent hashing for stable ownership.

2. Schemaless — a MySQL sharding and routing layer that exposes a simple key-value / table access pattern with automatic routing to the correct shard. Schemaless uses a mapping from shard-key -> shard and leverages Ringpop for routing and resilience.

Together, Ringpop + Schemaless allowed services to route traffic directly to the right host for a key, re-balance smoothly, and avoid global coordination during failures.

## Architecture (visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Service A Client] --> LB[Local Load Balancer / TChannel]
  LB --> RingpopClient[Ringpop Client (in-process)]
  RingpopClient --> ServiceNode1[Service Node 1]
  RingpopClient --> ServiceNode2[Service Node 2]
  ServiceNode1 --> SchemalessProxy[Schemaless Router]
  ServiceNode2 --> SchemalessProxy
  SchemalessProxy --> MySQLShard1[(MySQL Shard 1)]
  SchemalessProxy --> MySQLShard2[(MySQL Shard 2)]
  subgraph Gossip
    RingpopClient -- gossip --> ServiceNode1
    RingpopClient -- gossip --> ServiceNode2
  end
```

### How it flows

- A client needs the owner of key K. The in-process Ringpop instance computes the owner via consistent hashing — no remote RPC.
- The request is sent directly to the owner node (or a replica/forwarding node). If the owner is down, the ring quickly converges and ownership shifts.
- Schemaless maps application shard keys to specific MySQL shards and routes queries; Ringpop helps the router locate up-to-date shard replicas.

## Key design decisions & why they mattered

- Embedding membership in-process: eliminated a round trip for every routing decision. Local computation = low latency and no single point of failure.
- Consistent hashing: minimized key movement during re-shards; most keys stayed with the same node when hosts were added/removed.
- Gossip (SWIM) membership: fast failure detection and eventual convergence without centralized coordination.
- Keep MySQL: stick with proven relational DB, but add reliable routing (Schemaless) rather than rip-and-replace.
- Favor eventual consistency for membership and routing decisions to get high availability.

## Results (numbers Uber published / engineering outcomes)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------:|------:|
| Owner lookup cost | 1 RPC to central service + network (~ms range) | Local in-process compute (sub-ms) |
| Reconfiguration time on failures | Minutes (centralized reconfig) | ~seconds (gossip convergence) |
| Tail timeouts during failures | Frequent cascading timeouts | Much reduced; fewer cross-service retries |
| Operational complexity (shard moves) | Manual, touching many services | Automated, minimal key movement (consistent hash)

Concrete operational benefits reported by Uber engineers included: smoother scaling when adding nodes, fewer large-scale outages due to central-control-plane failures, and the ability to route high-throughput traffic directly to owners which cut end-to-end RPC p99s significantly in practice.

## Tradeoffs and pitfalls

- Eventual membership visibility: immediate consistency was sacrificed; there is a short window where different nodes disagree about ownership (mitigated by careful tuning).
- Application complexity: clients must handle forwarding or retries when ownership moves.
- Operational debugging: distributed ownership requires new tooling to inspect ring state and key ownership.

## Why this was a good match for Uber

- Uber needed nonlinear scale without a big control plane. Embedded consistent-hash rings gave that.
- They already relied on MySQL; Schemaless let them scale horizontally without rewriting storage.
- The approach maps well to the domain: many requests are keyed (rides, driver IDs, customer IDs) which fits consistent hashing and per-key routing.

### Short anecdote (engineer's view)

When a datacenter lost a rack mid-deploy, instead of a cascading failure that triggered central reconfiguration and hours of firefighting, the ring converged in seconds, ownership moved, and traffic continued — engineers only noticed a transient bump in latency. That one incident sold the approach.

## Practical checklist if you want to copy this pattern

- Use a gossip membership with health checks (SWIM-style) and tune failure timeouts conservatively for your environment.
- Choose a consistent hashing scheme with virtual nodes to reduce hot-spotting.
- Provide tools to inspect ring state and key -> owner mappings in real time.
- Ensure clients implement transparent retry/forwarding paths when an owner declines a request.
- If using relational DBs, implement a routing layer (like Schemaless) that maps application keys to shards; automate shard splits.

## Comparison: Before vs After (operational)

| Metric | Before (central discovery & manual shards) | After (Ringpop + Schemaless) |
|--------|-------------------------------------------:|-------------------------------:|
| Discovery latency | Central RPC per lookup (~1–5ms) | Local compute (<<1ms)
| Failure blast radius | Large (central service outages) | Localized to ring neighborhood
| Shard rebalancing | Manual, heavy key movement | Automated, low churn (consistent hash)
| Time to recover from node loss | Minutes | Seconds

## Closing

Ringpop + Schemaless show how a pair of pragmatic, focused systems can transform scale and reliability: local decisions, consistent hashing, and routing automation let engineers operate thousands of services and hundreds of thousands of RPCs/sec with lower operational overhead.
keyTakeaways: Embedding membership and routing in-process (Ringpop) removes central lookup bottlenecks and cuts lookup latency from ~ms to sub-ms.
Consistent hashing + virtual nodes minimizes key movement during resizes — reducing operational pain during re-shards and node churn.
Routing-layer approaches (Schemaless over MySQL) let you keep familiar storage systems while gaining horizontal scale and simpler operational workflows.

concept.title: Cache Like a Pro: Write-Through, Write-Back, and Cache-Aside Explained
concept.summary: Caching isn't just 'put it in Redis and pray' — it's a set of strategies with different trade-offs around latency, consistency, and write durability. Learn when to pick write-through, write-back, or cache-aside, see diagrams, before/after comparisons, and practical code snippets.
concept.explanation:
## Why this matters

Imagine a busy coffee shop with one barista (your database). When ten customers (requests) show up, the line grows and customers get upset. A cache is like a barista's assistant who hands out pre-made drinks for common orders — it reduces wait time. But what if orders change or the assistant gives the wrong drink? Caching strategies decide who keeps the canonical order and who is responsible for updates.

Picking the wrong caching strategy can cause stale reads, lost writes, or heavy complexity in recovery — all of which surface as bugs and outages in production.

---

## The core options (in plain English)

- **Cache-Aside (Lazy Load)**: Application reads from cache; on miss, it fetches from DB, returns it, and *populates the cache*. Writes go to the DB and (optionally) invalidate the cache.
- **Write-Through**: Application writes to the cache first; the cache synchronously writes the change to the backing store. Reads are always from cache.
- **Write-Back (Write-Behind)**: Application writes to cache; cache acknowledges immediately and asynchronously persists changes to the backing store later.

Think of them as: Cache-Aside = "ask kitchen if assistant doesn't have it". Write-Through = "tell assistant, they tell the kitchen before serving". Write-Back = "tell assistant, they make a note and deliver now, update kitchen later".

---

## How they work (mermaid flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  subgraph Read Path
    C[Client] --> A[App]
    A -->|Check Cache| R[Cache]
    R -->|Hit| H[Return Cached Value]
    R -->|Miss| DB[Database]
    DB -->|Value| A
    A -->|Populate Cache| R
    A -->|Return| H
  end

  subgraph Write-Aside (Cache-Aside)
    C2[Client Write] --> A2[App]
    A2 -->|Write DB| DB2[Database]
    DB2 -->|Invalidate/Update Cache| R2[Cache]
  end

  subgraph Write-Through
    C3[Client Write] --> A3[App]
    A3 -->|Write Cache| R3[Cache]
    R3 -->|Sync Write| DB3[Database]
  end

  subgraph Write-Back
    C4[Client Write] --> A4[App]
    A4 -->|Write Cache & Ack| R4[Cache]
    R4 -->|Async Persist| DB4[Database]
  end
```

---

## Sequence: Typical Cache-Aside read

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>App: GET /user/123
  App->>Cache: GET user:123
  Cache-->>App: MISS
  App->>DB: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=123
  DB-->>App: row
  App->>Cache: SET user:123 = row (expire)
  App-->>Client: 200 {user}
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER (single-cache introduction)

| Metric | Before (No Cache) | After (Cache-Aside with 95% hit) |
|--------|-------------------:|---------------------------------:|
| Avg latency (p50) | 25 ms | 6 ms |
| DB RPS | 10,000 | 500 |
| Error impact on DB overload | High | Much lower |
| Cache staleness risk | N/A | Depends on invalidation |

---

## Quick comparison table

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Cache-Aside | Simple, explicit control, no special cache-side logic required | App must handle misses & invalidation; more DB reads on first access | Read-heavy workloads, eventual consistency OK |
| Write-Through | Stronger read consistency (cache always up-to-date), simpler reads | Higher write latency (sync to DB), extra write path complexity | Read-mostly with moderate writes and need for low-latency reads |
| Write-Back | Very low write latency, reduces DB write load | Risk of lost data on cache crash; complex failure recovery; delayed persistence | High write throughput where occasional lag is acceptable and cache is durable/redundant |

---

## Practical examples, pitfalls & guidance

### Cache-Aside (most common)
- How: On read — try cache, on miss query DB, populate cache. On write — write DB then invalidate cache (or update cache).
- Good for: Read-heavy services (product catalog, user profiles), low write contention.
- Pitfalls:
  - *Cache stampede*: many requests on a cold key cause DB spike. Mitigate with request coalescing (singleflight), early warm-up, or locking per-key.
  - *Stale reads*: if you forget to invalidate/update cache after writes, clients see stale data.
  - *TTL tuning*: too long -> stale data; too short -> frequent DB hits.

Code (Node.js + ioredis) — cache-aside GET:

```javascript
// cache-aside GET
async function getUser(id) {
  const key = `user:${id}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(key);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  const user = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=$1', [id]);
  if (user) {
    await redis.set(key, JSON.stringify(user), 'EX', 60); // 60s TTL
  }
  return user;
}

// write path: update DB then invalidate
async function updateUser(id, patch) {
  await db.query('UPDATE users SET ... WHERE id=$1', [id]);
  await redis.del(`user:${id}`);
}
```

### Write-Through
- How: App writes to cache; cache synchronously writes to DB.
- Good for: Systems that require consistent reads immediately after writes and where write latency is acceptable.
- Pitfalls:
  - *Increased write latency* — every write must wait for DB.
  - *Single point of failure* if cache is the only front for writes; design for fail-open to DB if cache is unavailable.

### Write-Back (Write-Behind)
- How: Cache acknowledges write immediately, persists to DB asynchronously.
- Good for: Very high write throughput where short persistence delay is acceptable (e.g., analytics counters, session stores with durable backing)
- Pitfalls:
  - *Durability risk*: cache failure prior to persistence can lose data unless cache is replicated and persisted.
  - *Complex crash recovery*: need mechanisms to replay pending writes and maintain ordering.
  - *Write ordering*: concurrent writes may persist in wrong order if not handled.

---

## Operational practices & rules of thumb

- Start with *cache-aside* for most use cases — it's simple and safe.
- Use *write-through* when you need strong read-after-write consistency and can accept higher write latency.
- Use *write-back* only when write latency is critical and you can guarantee cache durability and robust recovery.
- Always plan for cache failures: your application must work correctly when cache is down (fall back to DB).
- Protect against stampedes: TTL jitter, request coalescing, early warming of popular keys.
- Monitor cache hit rate, eviction rate, and tail latencies. Low hit rate means wasted complexity.

---

## Small checklist before choosing a strategy

- How read-heavy is the workload? (If >90% reads, cache-aside can win)
- Do you need immediate read-after-write consistency? (Yes -> write-through)
- Can you accept some data-loss window for faster writes? (Yes -> write-back with durable cache)
- Can your cache persist writes or be highly redundant? (Needed for write-back)

---

## Final analogy

Think of the cache as an assistant in the coffee shop. Cache-aside = assistant fetches only when asked and remembers popular orders. Write-through = assistant confirms with kitchen before serving. Write-back = assistant serves immediately and tells kitchen later — fast, but risky if the assistant disappears.

Make the assistant's role explicit in your architecture, monitor them closely, and have a plan for when they call in sick.
keyTakeaways: Cache-aside is simple and safe for read-heavy workloads; handle cache invalidation and stampedes.
Write-through gives stronger read consistency but increases write latency.
Write-back can reduce write latency but requires durable cache and careful recovery to avoid data loss.
